metadata.title: How Light Works and Why It Controls What You Feel in Games
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: How Light Works and Why It Controls What
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Everything you see is rejection data, the wavelengths a surface bounces back rather than absorbs. Without deliberate lighting every rendered object risks reading as flat and lifeless. Real-time engines must hit 30 fps on console hardware with tightly limited memory. The Last of Us Part II designed dark, wet worlds so harsh sunlight would never look wrong.

props.artifactTitle: How Light Works - rejection as color
artifactLines: Everything you see is rejection data, the wavelengths a surface bounces back rather than absorbs.
Without deliberate lighting every rendered object risks reading as flat and lifeless.
Real-time engines must hit 30 fps on console hardware with tightly limited memory.
The Last of Us Part II designed dark, wet worlds so harsh sunlight would never look wrong.

beats.narration_text: The idea that everything you see is rejection data, and that The Last of Us Part II designed dark wet worlds so strong sunlight would not read as wrong, made real-time lighting click for me. Go deeper: (1) explain how absorbed versus reflected wavelengths become the color we perceive, (2) why every object risks looking flat without deliberate lighting, and (3) how a 30 fps console memory budget forces the art direction toward those overcast, rainy environments.

beats.narration_text: Every object risks looking flat. On-set lighting uses physical fixtures, modifiers, flags, and diffusion panels.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Every object risks looking flat. On-set lighting uses physical fixtures, modifiers, flags, and diffusion panels.

beats.narration_text: The whole system was built to hit 30 fps on console hardware with limited memory. The Last of Us Part II shipped a world designed around exactly this condition — dark, wet, post-apocalyptic environments where strong sunlight would read as wrong.
